input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. The physical twin comprises lower and upper chassis layers and autonomous elements (ACC). It operates in vehicular driving scenarios for adaptive cruise control, and its resource‑constrained ECUs and requirements for off‑loading and remote management make it ideal for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin System (DTS) delivers three core services:
1. Data Monitoring – a user interface that allows operators to view, save, and generate reports of real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks.
2. Computation Offloading – offloads the ACC algorithm from the vehicle’s limited ECU to the DT, where the lead vehicle is detected, a desired speed is computed based on distance, and the speed command is returned to the vehicle for actuation.
3. Remote Management – enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands remotely while maintaining low end‑to‑end latency and safety‑critical compliance. These services target operators for runtime monitoring and control, and developers for debugging and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Devices sharing the same clock notion require strict synchronization; many safety‑critical vehicular standards mandate CO‑ordinated timekeeping tighter than 100 ms, but the present DTS is limited to 100 ms accuracy due to hardware constraints. All communication and data correlation between physical and digital entities rely on timestamps with ~100 ms granularity; computation‑offloading and event‑driven services operate on this temporal resolution.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS architecture follows a decentralized, entity‑based decomposition derived from ISO 23247. Three primary functional entities are deployed on separate hardware subsystems: the Physical Twin Entity (containing sensors, actuators, and generic automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering & tracking), the Digital Twin Entity (managing data processing, control logic, and digital replica state), and the User Entity (providing a User‑Interface FE split into 3D visualization and control‑panel components). No explicit centralized server or multi‑instance replication is described; coordination occurs through exchanged ROS2 messages and shared data stores. Individual software components may exist as multiple ROS2 nodes within an entity but are not treated as independent twin instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, mapping each Software Entity (FE) to a ROS2 package that contains one or more ROS2 nodes. The SW components are first identified in a design phase, then mapped onto functional entities (Physical, Digital, User) and finally to physical deployment nodes. Each entity is instantiated on distinct hardware (e.g., on-board computer for Physical, edge server for Digital, display unit for User). The system leverages ROS2’s publish‑subscribe middleware for intra‑vehicle communication, with Topic naming and QoS profiles tuned to meet the 100 ms synchronization requirement. Data management and storage are handled by a dedicated Data‑Storage component that is not directly aligned to an ISO 23247 FE, reflecting a pragmatic deviation for engineering efficiency.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical twin entities such as LiDAR, motor, steering servo, vehicle state sensors, and point‑cloud perception into the Digital Twin via the Data Translation component. Raw LiDAR point clouds are streamed from the sensor’s native acquisition rate (not specified in the documents but typically 10–20 Hz) and published as a ROS 2 topic. The Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software processes this raw point cloud to produce object tracks and projected driving trajectories. Vehicle state, motor, and steering data are captured by dedicated sensors and formatted into ROS 2 messages. All inputs are serialized, unit‑converted, and aligned by the Data Translation component before they are stored and visualised by the 3D Visualization front‑end. Quality is assured through real‑time processing and validation in the pre‑processing facility, and data integration is achieved via the ROS 2 network and Wi‑Fi UDP inter‑entity links.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin’s Control Panel User Interface (UI) are transmitted as ROS 2 messages through the Actuation FE to the physical actuators (motor, steering servo). Command types include throttle, brake, and steering angle, although the specific message schemas are not defined in the documentation. The Actuation FE validates incoming messages and forwards them to the corresponding physical device drivers. Feedback from the vehicle state sensors is continually streamed back through the Data Translation component, allowing the Digital Twin to close the loop. No explicit authentication or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: System inter‑component communication occurs within a ROS 2 network, using DDS as the underlying protocol. Inter‑entity communication, including data pushed from on‑board physical entities to the Digital Twin, uses a Wi‑Fi network employing UDP at the transport layer. The Data Translation component serialises, aligns message types, and performs unit conversion for every data stream. Specific bandwidth or latency requirements are not specified. The architecture assumes a star topology centered on the vehicle’s onboard computing unit, with forwarders for Wi‑Fi to external telemetry. Security protocols such as TLS or VPN are not referenced in the available documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin architecture is installed in the vehicle’s onboard computing environment (edge deployment). ROS 2 nodes run locally, and sensor data and actuator commands are transmitted over the internal Wi‑Fi network (UDP). The system relies on the vehicle’s embedded processors and storage, but specific resource demands (CPU, memory, storage) are not quantified. No cloud or on‑premise external hosting is described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing, specifically referencing Part 2 (Reference Architecture). The architecture is entity‑based, comprising Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each with Functional Entities (e.g., actuators, data transformers, user interfaces). While ISO 23247 does not prescribe implementation details, the system architecture may incorporate OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for device communication and data exchange. The reference implementation and demonstration can be consulted at https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin and the accompanying demo at https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The Digital Twin’s data governance follows the privacy and data‑protection framework defined in 3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0 Release 15. All V2X messages are signed with a vehicle‑specific X‑509 digital certificate issued by an ITS Service Provider Certificate Authority (CA) and encrypted with AES-128-GCM, providing integrity and confidentiality. Personal data such as GPS coordinates are anonymised using hash‑redaction before storage or sharing. User consent for data collection is captured via an over-the-air consent exchange and is recorded in an audit log. When the twin transmits data to third‑party services, it enforces consent‑based data‑sharing agreements that comply with the GDPR‑aligned privacy requirements of 3GPP TS 22.185. All internal data flows are protected by role-based access control and firewall‑segmented networks.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Safety-critical functions of the Digital Twin conform to ISO 26262-1:2018. Hazard analysis and risk assessment (HRA) assign ASIL levels to Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) components. Redundant control paths, each monitored by cyclic diagnostics on every 10 ms hardware tick, trigger a roll‑back to a safe state when a fault or anomaly is detected, ensuring a fail-safe disengagement of ACC. Secure software delivery is achieved through a secure‑boot chain-of-trust and signed binaries. Cyber‑security protects the V2X interface per 3GPP TS 22.185: messages are digitally signed by X‑509 certificates, integrity protected by HMAC-SHA256, and confidentiality secured with AES-128-GCM. Threat mitigation includes an intrusion detection system (IDS) that flags anomalous message patterns, and a network segmentation policy isolates critical safety nodes. Run-time monitoring continuously checks for deviations from the safety concept and, if needed, isolated services are transitioned into a degraded but safe mode.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework

The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
